evals.prompt: I launched a support chatbot last week and I want to make sure it stays good over time. How do I set up monitoring so I get alerted if the quality drops?
evals.expected_output: A monitoring setup using dspy.Evaluate with a metric function (LM-as-judge or exact match), a degradation check comparing current scores against a baseline, and a scheduled evaluation loop. Code uses evaluator(program).score to get the numeric score from EvaluationResult.

assertions.name: uses_evaluate_correctly
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Evaluate (or from dspy.evaluate import Evaluate) and calls evaluator(program).score — not the bare return value — to extract the numeric score

assertions.name: has_baseline_comparison
assertions.description: Defines a baseline score dict and compares current scores against it to detect degradation, rather than just logging scores without comparison

assertions.name: uses_lm_as_judge_or_ground_truth_metric
assertions.description: Provides a metric function using either dspy.Predict with a judge Signature (for LM-as-judge) or exact/semantic match — metric instantiates the judge inside the function, not at module level

assertions.name: provider_agnostic
assertions.description: LM configuration shows at least one provider with a comment indicating alternatives (e.g., # or 'anthropic/...')

evals.prompt: We have a financial AI feature and regulators want ongoing proof it never gives investment advice or leaks PII. How do I build a safety monitoring system with DSPy?
evals.expected_output: A safety monitoring setup with multiple specialized metric functions (each checking a distinct policy violation), strict alert thresholds (e.g., >1% drop triggers alert), compliance logging to JSONL with pass/fail status, and a scheduled weekly check. Metrics use dspy.Predict with Signature classes for LM-based checks and regex for PII pattern matching.

assertions.name: separate_safety_metrics
assertions.description: Defines distinct metric functions for different safety concerns (e.g., no investment advice, no PII, no bias) rather than a single catch-all metric

assertions.name: strict_safety_threshold
assertions.description: Uses a stricter alert threshold for safety metrics (1% or lower) compared to quality metrics, reflecting zero-tolerance for safety regressions

assertions.name: compliance_logging
assertions.description: Logs evaluation results to persistent storage (JSONL, database, etc.) with timestamp and pass/fail status for audit trail purposes

assertions.name: judge_instantiated_in_metric
assertions.description: Any dspy.Predict judge used inside a metric function is instantiated inside that function (not at module level), to avoid thread-safety issues in parallel Evaluate runs

evals.prompt: My AI quality suddenly dropped — what does a DSPy monitoring setup look like that would have caught this earlier and also helps me recover?
evals.expected_output: A complete monitoring-to-recovery workflow: (1) periodic evaluation with dspy.Evaluate against a reference set, (2) degradation detection comparing against saved baseline, (3) alerting when threshold exceeded, (4) re-optimization with dspy.MIPROv2 using the same metric, (5) updating the baseline after successful re-optimization. Includes before/after score comparison.

assertions.name: end_to_end_recovery_flow
assertions.description: Shows the full loop: detect degradation -> re-optimize with MIPROv2 (or similar) using the same metric function -> re-evaluate -> update baseline

assertions.name: score_attribute_usage
assertions.description: All evaluator() calls use .score to extract the float from EvaluationResult (e.g., evaluator(program).score), not the raw return value

assertions.name: with_inputs_on_examples
assertions.description: eval set examples use .with_inputs() to mark input fields, preventing label leakage into the program during evaluation
